findings.description: N+1 query: low_stock_report() fires a separate COUNT(*) movements query per low-stock product inside the loop instead of a single LEFT JOIN/GROUP BY, producing tens of thousands of round-trips at scale.

The per-product COUNT(*) over movements filtered by sku + created_at requires a composite index on (sku, created_at) or it full-scans movements on every iteration, turning the N+1 into a quadratic scan (schema not in scope, so flagged as a dependency).
search_by_name() uses a leading-wildcard LIKE '%fragment%' which cannot use a B-tree index, forcing a full table scan of products on every admin-console search.

Every public method opens and tears down a fresh sqlite3 connection with no pooling or reuse, paying per-call open/close overhead in hot loops (lower impact for SQLite than a network DB, but still wasteful and loses prepared-statement/cache warmth).

low_stock_report() fetches the entire products table into a Python list and filters by reorder level in Python (line 81); pushing 'WHERE stock <= reorder_level' into SQL would avoid materializing the full catalog in memory and reduce data transfer.
Out-of-scope for perf but noted: f-string SQL interpolation of `fragment` into the LIKE clause is a SQL injection vector — defer detail to critic-security.
Out-of-scope for perf but noted: reserve() uses `stock > quantity` so an exact-stock reservation (stock == quantity) is rejected; likely should be `>=` — defer to critic-logic.
